items.q: What is the difference between area and perimeter?
items.correct: Perimeter is the total distance around the outside; area is the amount of surface inside (measured in square units).
items.misconception: Area and perimeter are different names for the same measurement.
items.random_wrong: Area is half the perimeter for any shape.
items.why: Students confuse the two terms because both describe the size of a shape, but they don't see that one is one-dimensional (length) and the other two-dimensional (length squared).

items.q: What is the relationship between the angle at the centre and the angle at the circumference subtended by the same arc?
items.correct: The angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference.
items.misconception: The angles are equal because they are subtended by the same arc.
items.random_wrong: The angle at the centre is half the angle at the circumference.
items.why: Students intuitively expect 'same arc = same angle' and don't internalise that the central angle is double the inscribed angle.

items.correct: No — a prime number has exactly two distinct factors (1 and itself); 1 has only one factor.
items.misconception: Yes — 1 has no factors except itself, which makes it prime.
items.random_wrong: Yes — 1 is the smallest prime number.
items.why: Students apply a loose 'only divisible by 1 and itself' rule and miss the precise requirement of exactly two distinct factors.
It's 17. “15” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: 36. “12” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: For a fair die roll X, find E(X) (give to 1 dp).
items.why: Exact answer: 3.5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
